rows.v: Prosciutto di Parma, Prosciutto di San Daniele, Jamón Serrano, Jamón Ibérico, Speck, Jambon de Bayonne, Culatello, Country Ham
Sliced paper-thin and served simply — let the cure's flavor speak for itself.
The finest Jamón Ibérico comes from black-hoofed pigs fattened on acorns — 'pata negra' — and a single leg can age for years before it's ready to slice.
Coppa, Lomo, Pancetta, Guanciale, Bresaola, Cecina, Lardo
Sliced thin as a starter — pairs beautifully with crusty bread and pickles.
Guanciale — not bacon or pancetta — is the traditional cut for a real carbonara or amatriciana: cured pork jowl, richer and more delicate than belly.
Milano Salami, Napoli Salami, Genoa Salami, Soppressata, Finocchiona, Ventricina
A board staple — their fat content means they pair well with acidic pickles and wine.
Finocchiona was born of thrift — when black pepper was rare and costly in Tuscany, makers reached for cheap, local fennel, and the fennel salami became a classic.
Chorizo, Fuet, Salchichón, Sobrasada
Often grilled or pan-fried (fresh varieties) or sliced thin (cured) — bold, paprika-forward flavors.
What makes chorizo red isn't chili but pimentón — Spanish smoked paprika — which arrived from the New World in the 16th century and reinvented Spanish curing.
Saucisson Sec, Rosette de Lyon, Saucisse de Morteau, Saucisse de Montbéliard
Air-dried and ready to slice — a classic apéritif companion with cornichons and mustard.
Morteau sausage is smoked in a 'tuyé,' a tall pyramid farmhouse chimney, over conifer wood — and sealed at one end with a little wooden peg that proves it's the real thing.
Pâté de Campagne, Rillettes, Nduja, Pâté en Croûte, Terrine, Fromage de Tête
Spread on toasted bread or crackers — pair with something acidic to cut the richness.
'Fromage de tête' — head cheese — contains no cheese at all; it's meat from a whole simmered pig's head set in its own savory jelly.
Mortadella, Bacon, Galantine, Boudin Noir, Kielbasa, Frankfurter
Ready to eat as-is or with minimal cooking — the most approachable entry point on the board.
The word 'charcuterie' comes from the French for 'cooked flesh,' and the humble hot dog is a direct descendant — a finely emulsified, smoked sausage in the grand charcutier tradition.
